data.title: Can AI Chip Design Help Baidu And Alibaba Unlock Shareholder Value?
data.description:
By Henry Greene

In the age of AI, hardware is crucial. Large language models (LLMs) and other AI systems demand extreme amounts of computing power. Seemingly, the only limitation to their development is the quantity and quality of the chips that they can access.

The valuations of the world’s largest chip designers and manufacturers are a testament to the importance of chips to AI development. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing ('TSMC')(TSM) is a key manufacturer, or fabricator, of the high-end microchips that AI systems run on and has seen its share price increase 429% since ChatGPT launched in November. Meanwhile, Nvidia (NVDA), a key chip designer, also known as a fabless chipmaker, has seen its share price rise 1,270% since ChatGPT was launched.

Facing an uncertain supply of high-end chips due to US export restrictions under the Biden administration, Alibaba (BABA) and Baidu (BIDU) invested heavily in their own chip design capabilities. Today, these ventures have the potential to help them unlock significant shareholder value. A simple Sum of the Parts (SOTP) analysis of Baidu and Alibaba, including their respective chip design arms, Kunlunxin and T-Head, suggests markets may not be valuing these internet giants properly. Although chip design accounts for a small portion of revenue for both companies, if these businesses could command the same valuation multiples as global peers like Nvidia or China-based fabless peers like Cambricon, both businesses would be worth significantly more.

Sum of the Parts (SOTP) Analysis of Baidu & Alibaba

Baidu and Alibaba represent full-stack AI ecosystems, generating value across models, cloud, and now semiconductors. These capabilities were added on top of existing businesses in advertising and e-commerce for Baidu and Alibaba, respectively, making these companies conglomerates. Baidu’s new technological edge is also added on top of a relatively mature robotaxi business: Apollo Go.

Starting with Baidu, we conducted a sum-of-the-parts analysis to find the implied market valuation of its businesses, derived from Baidu's overall market capitalization and assumptions about these businesses' contribution to overall revenue. Baidu's chip design unit, Kunlunxin, and its robotaxi unit, Apollo Go, appear undervalued compared to the valuations the markets are currently applying to comparable businesses Cambricon and Waymo for chip design and robotaxis,2 respectively.

Alibaba appears to be in a similar situation with at least one of its business units. Its chip design unit, T-Head, if valued at a fraction of Alibaba's market cap proportionate to its revenue contribution, is also worth considerably less than Cambricon.

Exploring The Differences Between T-Head & Kunlunxin

T-Head and Kunlunxin are both fabless semiconductor companies, meaning they do not manufacture their own chips. Rather, they are customers of fabricators such as TSMC, focusing instead on chip design. Kunlunxin is estimated to have generated $500 million in revenue in 2025.3 Its revenue mix is currently split evenly between external customers and chips for internal use within Baidu’s massive cloud server network. China Mobile, one of China’s largest mobile phone carriers, is a key customer. The mobile provider was also a key strategic investor in the unit, valuing it at $3 billion when it invested in a funding round in mid-2025.3 Kunlunxin's product focus is purely on AI accelerators, specialized chips meant to speed up inference operations.

T-Head is estimated to have generated $1.5 billion in revenue in 2025. Like Kinlun, T-Head benefits from both external and internal demand. However, T-Head receives approximately 60% of its revenue from external customers, whereas Kunlunxin only receives 50% of its revenue from its external customers.

Although both Kunlunxin and T-Head have developed niches and established strong domestic, internal, and external consumption pipelines for their products, the status of US export controls may affect their performance. China approved a first batch of H200 Nvidia chips for ByteDance (BDNCE), Alibaba, and Tencent (TCEHY) in mid-January this year, after the Trump Administration departed from the previous US Administration’s stance of completely restricting the export of higher-end AI chips. However, the extent to which Nvidia chips will be available in China remains uncertain, and Kunlunxin and T-Head could step in to make up for lost demand when necessary. The continued uncertainty of overseas chip availability to China-based customers remains an opportunity for these players, alongside Cambricon.

Potential Spin-Off Scenarios

Baidu has already filed for a Hong Kong IPO of Kunlunxin. However, Alibaba has not indicated any intended change for T-Head within its corporate structure.

Below are three possible spin-off scenarios for Kunlunxin and T-Head.

Scenario #1: The parent companies could choose to keep the IPO float low and maintain a large stake, potentially a controlling one, in the business. If the spinoffs are in high demand with investors, this is an ideal strategy, as the companies would benefit most from the potential appreciation of the floated shares. This would be similar to how JD.com (JD) maintained a 50% ownership stake in its own logistics business after spinning it off as JD Logistics (JDLGF) through a Hong Kong IPO in 2021.4

Scenario #2: The parent companies could float the entirety of their fabless chipmaking units on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange and not keep a meaningful stake. We believe this is unlikely, as the units are relatively small and will find it difficult to attract sufficient outside investor demand to lead to the valuations mentioned above on their own. This would lead to a potential one-time windfall for the companies’ shareholders, which could be distributed to investors as a dividend or be reinvested into their businesses. Given Baidu’s currently high cash position, we believe the company would likely decide to distribute the windfall from the IPO as a dividend.

Scenario #3: Alibaba could decide not to follow in Baidu’s footsteps and keep its T-head unit wholly owned inside the company. This would limit the increase in its share price, though market sentiment around chip IPOs stemming from Baidu’s spinoff may potentially raise Alibaba’s valuation if its revenue share increases. The company could still decide to pursue an internal restructuring, thereby calling out T-Head as a key component of its business and breaking out its revenue consistently. Alibaba pursued a similar strategy in 2023 by breaking itself into six businesses without any spin-offs. This may have triggered investors to look at the company through the lens of an SOTP analysis. Alibaba’s stock has risen over 80% since that restructuring.²

Conclusion

Alibaba and Baidu are full-stack AI value generators if you include their chipmaking units in a holistic analysis of their businesses. A sum-of-the-parts analysis suggests that these units are undervalued relative to comparable businesses. Spinning off their chipmaking units, or at least forcing the market to value them separately, could generate significant shareholder value.

Citations:

  1. Butts, Dylan. "Baidu's semiconductor unit Kunlunxin files for Hong Kong listing amid AI chip boom in China," CNBC. January 2, 2026.
  2. Reuters. "Waymo Seeking About $16 Billion near $110 Billion Valuation, Bloomberg News Reports," Reuters. January 31, 2026.
  3. Company report as of 12/31/2025.
  4. "PROPOSED SPIN-OFF AND SEPARATE LISTING OF JD LOGISTICS INC. ON THE MAIN BOARD OF THE STOCK EXCHANGE OF HONG KONG LIMITED," JD.com Inc., HKEX News. February 16, 2021.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vodafone: Rotation 2 Months Ago, 'HOLD' In 2026
data.description:
I was one of the few analysts on SA who, when it was cheap, was positive on Vodafone (VOD) as an investment. At the time, about a year ago, it represented my by far largest telco holding. However, following a truly stellar climb to heights not seen for some time, I went "HOLD" on my rating and chose to rotate my entire stake in the company a few months back - a few weeks before starting my IG, Wolf of Value.

This should not be viewed as something I have against Vodafone, but rather something having to do with the company valuation, which at the time, unfortunately, had expanded to very high levels. I bought the company at less than £0.7/share for the native ticker trading on the London Stock Exchange, and my cost basis was even lower than that, even post the dividend cut, generating a very attractive dividend yield.

As it stands now, following my recent article found here, the company has performed in accordance with and alongside my expectations. I rated it at a "HOLD" at an ADR share price of about $15.1/share - the company has underperformed the SPY since that time, as you'll see in the infographic below.

Vodafone has been doing a lot of things right, as I also covered in that same article. Everything from successful mergers to growth markets offsetting legacy has been justifying this company's valuation increases. But at my latest article, the justification for higher P/E premiums stopped. As I am writing this article, Vodafone doesn't offer even a 3.4% native yield, and is "only" BBB rated, with a P/E of close to 15x. There is a decent forecast for growth for this company going forward, but me, I consider that growth to be very unlikely at this time, and consider cost pressures, inflation, and unfavorable comps to deliver a more moderate increase.

Why that is, I will tell you here.

Vodafone - Significant upside only if growth materializes, which I view as very unlikely

Let's get down to brass tacks straightaway. Vodafone is currently estimating a multi-year growth upside for the period 2028-2029E. The company is betting on a number of things to deliver this AEPS growth. First off, the Three UK merger. The expectation for the company's deal to compete with BT and O2 is expected to bring in excess of half a billion pounds of cost synergies every single year - and this estimated materialization starts in 2028, so in about 2 years. However, I consider these supposed merger synergies to be unrealistic at their current estimate. This is because of existing regulatory constraints and a chronic overestimation of cost savings. This is not just something Vodafone does - many companies do it. To give you an example, VOD means to merge about 10,000 redundant cell sites, including IT systems such as billing and the like. The complexity of such wind-downs and merging of systems often results in cost additions as well as savings, diluting the eventual goal of what it's trying to do.

Furthermore, Vodafone claimed £11B of investments for 10 years to come, once the merger is approved. The details of how these CapEx spends are going to flow are unclear - and I would refer you back to the 4-to-3 merger, such as India and Idea, for failures in exactly this. This specific move not only resulted in massive net customer add losses, but also failed to produce any sort of financial stability in the company.

Secondly, continental Europe. Vodafone has been selling off what it considers to be non-core continental assets and operations, many of them in geographies that I would personally consider to be somewhat troubled. This includes, above all, Italy and Spain. Instead, VOD is favoring higher-growth and higher-market-share geographies. When it comes to this particular part of the strategy, I cannot really argue with it - though there are some risks with doing it. As a result of the sale, Vodafone's footprint shrinks, and the footprint is a reason that Vodafone commands a fair premium over other telcos. It reflects an ignorance of scale, of revenue growth potential, which is somewhat baffling since the company actually has a strong focus on other growth markets. So, something I look at a bit dubiously at.

Selling off non-core, however, does not solve some of Vodafone's issues - including low sales growth in relation to CapEx. This weighs on all telcos, but Vodafone has seen some extreme amounts of it.

What I am presenting to you here is a case against why Vodafone will grow as expected, because the current AEPS growth estimates are extremely "lofty", to say the least. 2028E is expected to bring about 25%, and 2029E is expected to bring 50% of AEPS, with a total AEPS of upwards of £0.15/share. If that were to materialize, the company could even go back to its old dividends.

There are reasons aside from what I say above that this is unrealistic, however.

First off, the company is not apt at hitting targets historically. It quite often misses them.

Furthermore, and more importantly, none of the company's remaining European markets, excluding the UK, is likely to drive the sort of growth that would enable these sorts of returns. Vodafone expects Germany to return to significant growth. Germany is not a growth market for telcos - Deutsche Telekom (DTEGY) is proof of this. It may grow at low single digits, but low single digits isn't going to be contributing the necessary upside to hit those targets. The company's focus on improving ARPU rather than just chasing net adds is laudable, but hardly unique or new. I don't see any significant reason as to why Vodafone should succeed more than its competition when it comes to this.

While African growth and AI-driven cost efficiencies are likely to be a better driver of forward upside than the above-mentioned ones, they also won't move the needle as much as seems to be expected here. Automation and AI come with system and upkeep costs, and OpenRAN comes with its own challenges. Where I actually see the clearest and most obvious upside for Vodafone is its increased capital structure improvements. Lower debt and interest will make a clear case for lower cost, which increases earnings. But again, not as much as this.

The totality of all of these upsides will, as I see it, not come close to the upside that investors seem to be seeing or citing here. It will become (quite likely) a leaner operator, improving every market. But I argue its AEPS improvements will be in the high single-digit to low double-digit range, rather than low to mid-range.

This means that any PT above a level that assumes this would be too high, especially with what is at over £1.1/share, a very conservative yield indeed for a telecommunications company, one that is in fact under the risk-free rate.

Vodafone valuation - without the AEPS growth, the annualized RoR is sub-10%

I forecast Vodafone using the 20-year average of about 12.5x, which is also in line with other telcos on the market. I give the company, for the sake of argument and positivity, a 9% annualized AEPS growth every single year until 2029.

Using that 12.5x P/E, and expecting an AEPS just below, or at £0.1/share, comes to no more than an implied price target of about £1.15/share for 2029, as the current blended P/E is closer to 16x. That means that assuming a 12.5x P/E average, and assuming even close to 10% growth per year, the annualized rate of return would be less than 7% - and that is with the 3% dividend. Without the dividend, the RoR would be closer to 2.8% per year.

That is insufficient for me, and I believe that it should be insufficient for you as well, on the basis of risk-free rates giving over 4% per year at this time.

My stance continues to be that the company is priced too high. I believe its current share price is a product of its substantial price hikes, in some cases about 4% above CPI in the UK, meaning Vodafone is pushing above-inflation price hikes. This is not necessarily a positive, because these hikes imply that the company may be able to repeat this feat. It is, however, quite unlikely. We've been looking at a 70% recovery in Vodafone in a single year. This speaks to me making the right decision from a valuation perspective, but that, in my view, also includes the right decision to "get out" of the investment at a higher price.

In my last article, I gave the company a PT of about £0.95/share. I consider this, in fact, to even be a bit high here, but I'm sticking to my target, because I do believe that the company is able to do some major improvements to its balance sheet. Note that this does mean that the company is now more than 15% overvalued, and that I would continue to view it as a "HOLD" here.

The recent "flat trading" in the stock confirms to me that I made the right decision here, and I'm waiting to "get back in" at this time, wanting an upside of at least 15% per year.

In my IG, Wolf of Value, I cover other telcos similar to VOD that I consider to be more attractive than this one, as well as other interesting investments for both conservative, income-oriented investors, as well as more growth-oriented investors.

Risks & Considerations

The biggest risk with Vodafone is now in the medium term, now that short-term risks have been somewhat overcome. The combination of continued macro pressure compounded with volatility in its markets tells me that the company has come as far as it can go.

Aside from fundamental risks to its geographies, I view Vodafone's current risk profile as being impacted by overreliance on the German segment for stable revenues. Despite investments, multiple telcos have shown us that finding growth or even stability in Germany is very hard. I do not view the growth markets as the company's main problems, if things happen there, but the impacts of a lower or anemic return in its legacy. I don't think it would necessarily hurt Vodafone over the short term, but I do believe the market reaction would be to lower the share price, and I maintain this view as of April 2026.

In this case, and as of this article update, Vodafone is a "HOLD". My position, as it stands, is rotated.

Thesis

Vodafone is a qualitative, Europe-covering telco with an African and geographical growth vector. The company has a decent yield, though much lower now than before. It no longer has the sort of upside that I would be interested in investing in.

The company is no longer cheap or attractive here at more than £1/share and at close to 15x P/E. The risk/reward consideration needs to be looked at in conjunction with things like the 2026E forecast, which is negative. I no longer see market-beating upside, and for that reason, the company becomes a "HOLD", with my recent article marking the point where I sold my stake.

My updated PT for the company is £0.95, meaning a "HOLD", This comes to about $11.45/share for the ADR VOD.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is not enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a valuation target of $11.45/share, I don't see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I rate the company a "HOLD" rating as of February 2026 and as of Q3 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: An Important Lesson For Investors
data.description:
Originally published on April 15, 2026

Just over two weeks ago, on March 30th, every single key US index ETF that we track in our Trend Analyzer tool was oversold, with all but the Dow 30 ETF in extreme oversold territory.

Fear was permeating, and bearish sentiment gauges were rising sharply.

Fast forward to two weeks later. As shown below, these same index ETFs are now all in overbought territory (with the exception of the Dow 30).

For investors and traders, the action over the last few weeks is a good reminder that markets don’t stay oversold or overbought forever.

If you’re fearful when prices get oversold or ebullient when prices get overbought, try to remind yourself that the script will eventually flip.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Money Supply Growth In 2026 Rises To Multi-Year High As The Fed Pumps New QE
data.description:
By Ryan McMaken

In spite of more than four years of claiming that price inflation was transitory, and that it would quickly return to the Fed’s two-percent target, the Fed has insisted on more easy-money policy over the past 18 months. In that time, the Fed has cut the target interest rate by 175 basis points and has returned to quantitative easing through monthly $40 billion purchases of Treasurys.

Through it all, Fed Chairman Jerome Powell has repeatedly described monetary policy using some variation of “restrictive.” In his most recent FOMC press conference, for example, Powell described the Fed’s policy as “borderline restrictive” or “modestly restrictive.”

To be fair, this suggests a slight movement away from Powell’s earlier dubbing of US monetary policy as “relatively restrictive” late last year, and as “clearly restrictive” before that.

Indeed, it may be that current policy is “restrictive” compared to, say, the policies of Bernanke and Yellen. But recent data on the money supply suggests that the money supply in recent months is finding plenty of room to increase rapidly, in spite of what Fed officials say. All these claims of restrictive monetary policy fail to ring true when we look at actual movement in the money supply.

For example, the money supply has increased every month for five of the past six months. Moreover, when measured year-over-year, the money supply has accelerated over the past three months and is now at the highest rate of growth seen in 44 months—or since June of 2022.

While the money supply largely flatlined through much of mid-2025, growth has clearly accelerated since August of last year and has clearly been trending upward since June of 2023.

During February, year-over-year growth in the money supply was at 5.56 percent. That’s up from January’s year-over-year increase of 4.90 percent. Money supply growth is also up sizably compared to February of last year when year-over-year growth was 2.39 percent.

In February, the total money supply again rose, heading above $20.4 trillion and growing by a trillion dollars in seven months from July 2025 to February 2026. Although the total money supply fell throughout much of 2023 and 2024, the money supply has nonetheless increased by $1.6 trillion since that time, and is now only half a trillion below the covid-era peak.

Measuring month-to-month growth, we find eight of the last twelve months with increases to the money supply, including substantial growth during November 2025, December 2025 and February of this year. August, September, and October all posted some of the largest growth rates we’ve seen since 2022.

The money supply metric used here - the “true,” or Rothbard-Salerno, money supply measure (TMS) - is the metric developed by Murray Rothbard and Joseph Salerno, and is designed to provide a better measure of money supply fluctuations than M2.

Historically, M2 growth rates have often followed a similar course to TMS growth rates, but throughout much of 2025, M2 outpaced even TMS, and M2 money-supply totals are again rapidly heading upward. M2 is now at the highest level it’s ever been, topping $22.5 trillion. TMS has not yet returned to its 2022 peak but is now at a 39-month high.

Since 2009, the TMS money supply is now up by more than 206 percent. (M2 has grown by more than 160 percent in that period.) Out of the current money supply of $20.4 trillion, nearly 30 percent of that has been created since January 2020.

Since 2009, in the wake of the global financial crisis, more than $14 trillion of the current money supply has been created. In other words, more than two-thirds of the total existing money supply have been created since the Great Recession.

Given current weak economic conditions, it is surprising to see such robust growth in the money supply. For example, the estimate for GDP growth in the fourth quarter of 2025 was recently downgraded to 0.5 percent. And job growth since late 2025 has been either weak or negative, depending on which measure of employment we use.

The Fed now concludes that job growth going into 2026 is essentially zero. This is likely reflected in the fact that consumer confidence has apparently fallen to a multi-decade low.

Given all this, we would not expect to see such robust growth in the money supply. Private commercial banks play a large role in growing the money supply in response to loose Fed policy.

When economic conditions are expansive, and as employment grows, lending also grows, further loosening monetary conditions. But when economic conditions are weak, we’d expect to see less lending and bank-fueled monetary growth.

So, we should expect to see downward pressure on money supply growth given current economic conditions. However, in an effort to further pump asset prices and somehow counter our growing economic stagnation, the Fed again ratcheted up Fed Treasury purchases - paid with newly created money - and even lowered the target policy interest rate again in December.

This continuation of accommodative monetary policy - which belies Fed claims of “restrictive” policy - has surely done its part in returning the money supply to growth levels we haven’t seen in years.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: April 2026 Trading Outlook: Fiscal Flows, Oil, Bank Credit, And Fed Interest Rates
data.description:
Introduction

This report assesses risk‑asset performance through the combined lenses of sectoral balances, the real estate cycle, and fiscal‑flow dynamics. With inflation easing and unemployment beginning to rise, the interaction between an increasingly reactive Federal Reserve and renewed fiscal expansion is becoming more consequential—particularly against the backdrop of ongoing geopolitical shocks to global energy markets.

Sectoral Balances Overview

The table below tracks monthly shifts in sectoral balances, velocity, and acceleration—core signals for reading market direction.

Fiscal Flow Breakdown

February is seasonally a strong month for fiscal flows and March was always going to be weaker and therefore field negative red velocity and acceleration results.

Going into detail: The $199+ billion private sector funds surplus came from a moderate $167 billion injection of funds from the federal government. This injection was augmented by a healthy $87+ billion of credit creation by the banking sector and a debit of an estimated $-55 billion from the external sector.

Note that with the release of updated balance of payments numbers the external deficit numbers have been revised downwards for several of the preceding months and this has resulted in better than expected financial results.

Market Implications

The chart below plots the sectoral balance data in rate‑of‑change terms. It reflects federal spending (G) plus the external sector (X, typically negative), showing the net flow of funds into or out of the private domestic sector (P)—an accounting identity by definition.

Last month the model predicted fortunes would change. When the green line (private sector balance) rises strongly from a low base markets tend to finish the month higher as well. While the month finished deeply in the red at the close of the month, if you wind the clock forward just two weeks later the market has returned to all time new highs in an epic snap back retrace that started on March 30 and had reached all time new highs by April 15.

This model is not granular enough to predict a rally to the day however the general thrust is correct, a rally was predicted and did eventuate and was far stronger than expected.

What this shows though is that the stock market still has a lot of strength and conviction left in it despite that we are coming to the end of the real estate cycle.

Fiscal Policy Outlook

The table below details federal government withdrawals from Treasury accounts at the Federal Reserve. Because these outflows are credited to the private sector, they represent a positive liquidity impulse for asset markets.

Federal outlays in March were strong at over $3.5T and almost set another all-time high. The deficit itself was relatively smaller as most of the expenditure was drained back out again via bonds, taxes and other fees.

Fiscal milestones coming up:

There is now the largest federal tax collection about to take place in the middle of April where about $200 billion will be drained out of the private sector.

This will be the seasonal high for the Treasury Cash Balance [TCB]. Made high by the tax receipts. The rising TCB is shown in the chart below and is known there as the TGA or Treasury General Account.

This may cause the SPX to retrace again from its newly achieved all time high, make a higher low and then move higher still as the month progresses.

Monetary Policy Outlook

At its March 2026 meeting, the Federal Reserve opted to keep rates steady, maintaining the federal funds rate in the 3.5%–3.75% target range.

One big change has been the restarting of quantitative easing [QE]. The black, blue and orange lines on this chart are now rising as the Fed's assets grow. In lockstep the reserves held at US banks rise [green line] as they swap bonds for cash.

The next meeting is this month on the 28-29th. Rates will probably stay the same though there were arguments for them to come down but the Iran war and the impact of higher oil prices has changed the interest rate landscape and is discussed below.

One area the Fed looks at the most is the housing market given that over 70% of the capital structure consists of housing mortgages. The chart below shows the US housing market put in another new all time high in January, so no need for concern at present. The housing index is forecast to keep rising until at least the middle of this year and is doing so but at a slower rate.

The chart above suggests the US housing sector is in an active, ongoing contraction — not approaching one. With mortgage rates still elevated and leading indicators (permits/starts) deeply negative, a genuine recovery likely requires either a significant rate cut or a demand shock neither of which is likely.

Fed Rate Stance

The chart below plots the key indicators most useful for anticipating the Fed’s next move—signals that have historically been highly predictive.

  • Red Line (1Y % change of S&P 500): This line is trending upward. This suggests strong stock market momentum, which often precedes economic resilience but can also signal future inflationary pressure.
  • Light Green Line (1Y % change of CPI): This represents inflation. It is shown drifting lower from its peaks, supporting the idea that the Fed can stop raising rates because price increases are cooling.
  • Pink Line (1Y actual change of US Treasury 10YR): This shows the momentum of long-term interest rates. It is volatile but trending downward, indicating that the "market" expects lower rates ahead.
  • Dark Green Line (1Q growth rate of GDP): This shows economic output. It is relatively flat to slightly down, suggesting a cooling economy that doesn't require further rate hikes to "slow down."
  • Purple Line (US Treasury 10 Year Yield): This is the actual yield. It is shown stabilizing and beginning to decline, mirroring the forecast for the FFR.
  • Orange/Brown Line (US Unemployment - Initial Claims): This tracks joblessness. It shows a slight upward drift, which typically signals a weakening labor market—another reason the Fed would likely stop raising rates.
  • Black Line (1Q % change of Nonfarm Payrolls): This measures job creation. It is trending toward the zero line, indicating a significant slowdown in hiring.

Up until the recent Iran war oil price shock the economy was weakening and requiring rate cuts to make fiscal space for growth as more income is directed to higher land prices, rent and loan repayments rather than to productive asset investment. Now the lower rate scenario has been put on hold.

The recent SPX recovery rally indicates the market thinks the worst is over whereas the bond market is signalling caution with higher yields.

Global Macro Trends

At the world macroeconomic level, we have the G5 chart below.

The chart above tracks the relationship between global liquidity (how much money is being pumped into the system) and the prices of risk assets like stocks and crypto.

The main takeaway is that global government and central bank spending acts as a "carrier wave" for markets, while China's credit cycle provides the short-term timing for when these markets might pivot

Liquidity & Macro Drivers

  • Solid Sky Blue Line (C5 Central Banks' Aggregate Balance Sheet): Represents the total assets held by the central banks of the US, Eurozone, Japan, UK, and China. When this line rises, central banks are injecting liquidity (printing money), which historically pushes asset prices higher. This line is advanced 18 months,
  • Brown Line (1Y % Change of G5 Government Expenditure): Shows the yearly growth in government spending across major economies. The chart labels this as the "carrier wave" for global financial liquidity, suggesting that fiscal policy (government budgets) is a foundational driver of market trends. This line is advanced 18 months.
  • Dashed Green Line (China's Credit Impulse, High Frequency): A leading indicator that measures the flow of new credit in China as a percentage of GDP. It is highly cyclical (usually 3–3.5 years) and often predicts shifts in global manufacturing and risk appetite about 6 months in advance.

Risk Assets (Market Performance)

  • Black Line (MSCI World U$): Tracks a broad index of global stocks from developed markets. Notice how it often moves in tandem with the liquidity lines, especially following peaks or troughs in China's credit impulse.
  • Purple Line (Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF): This is a proxy for Bitcoin's price. Crypto is highly sensitive to changes in global liquidity ("easy money"), which is why it often shows more extreme swings than the broader stock market.
  • Pink & Orange Lines (4W Lag of USD to Bitcoin/Ether): These lines show the price of Bitcoin and Ethereum with a 4-week delay. This "lag" is used to see if previous shifts in liquidity (the "carrier wave") are accurately predicting current price movements.

The chart above has the lines advanced with an 18-month lead for G5 government expenditures and the C5 central bank balance sheets and suggest a major shift in global liquidity.

Key Macro Forecast Indicators for 2026–2028

  • Inflection Point in 2026: The chart indicates that the "Carrier Wave" of liquidity (the brown G5 line) peaks in early-to-mid 2026. This often precedes a peak in risk assets, such as the MSCI World Index and Bitcoin, by several months.
  • Declining Liquidity in 2027: Following the peak, the advanced G5 and C5 lines show a sharp downward trajectory throughout 2026 and into 2027. This historical correlation suggests a significant tightening of global financial systemic liquidity, which typically puts downward pressure on asset prices.
  • Risk Asset Correction: Since these indicators tend to lead the market, the forecast implies that risk assets may enter a bearish phase or see a substantial correction starting in late 2026 and accelerating through 2027, tracking the projected drop in government and central bank support.
  • Potential Bottom in 2027: The G5 balance sheet line (brown line) shows signs of stabilizing or reaching a localized floor by early 2027. If the leading relationship holds, this could point toward a market recovery or "bottoming out" of risk asset prices sometime in 2027 and a rally into 2028.

The chart positions China’s Credit Impulse (green dashed line) as the provider of short-term "inflection points." As of April 2026, the volatility in this line suggests that while there may be short-term bounces, the broader trend is dictated by the massive G5/C5 "Carrier Wave," which is currently forecasting a period of liquidity withdrawal.

Conclusion & Investment Outlook

The 20-year seasonal averages shown in the chart above point to markets falling lower into the middle of March due to the lagged effect of weak fiscal flows caused by back to back Federal taxation in December and January.

When compared to what has actually happened this year (lower chart) so far it has been very accurate. What has been very surprising is the strength with which the stock market recovered back to all time highs after months of decline. The rally came at the end of March rather than the middle and even though the trough was amplified deeper by the Iran war bad sentiment it shot back up in record time at the end of the month. There would be a notional pause or retrace now due to April tax and then it is onward and upwards into May.

At some stage demand destruction from $100+ oil must take its toll however this is a lagged effect that has yet to make itself felt.

On the chart above the green line a proxy for oil has shot off the chart. If it stays elevated long enough the other co factor will follow it upward.

We are currently at the final "fragility phase" of the real estate cycle. With global transaction volumes already softening in Q1 2026 and the 10-year yield resetting structurally higher, the property market has lost its buffer.

  • The Squeeze: Higher oil prices [green line] feed directly into construction costs (diesel, bitumen, PVC, transport).

  • The Thesis: In a late-real estate cycle environment, developers can no longer pass these costs onto buyers who are already tapped out by high mortgage rates. This leads to a "Development Freeze"—the classic precursor to a construction-led recession.

The market's initial reaction to the February 2026 blockade was a price spike, but the recessionary impact is determined by persistence.

  • The Threshold: Historical modeling (Oxford Economics) suggests that oil needs to average $115–$125/bbl for at least one full quarter to trigger a global GDP contraction.

  • The Forecast: With Brent projected to average $115/bbl through Q2 2026, we are currently crossing that "red line." If the Strait remains restricted into June, the lag effect suggests a Q3/Q4 recession onset. This would time for the trough in G5 flows shown in the third chart up.

    Central banks are in a corner and have a critical role to play:

  • The Conflict: If they cut rates to save the economy from high energy costs, they risk de-anchoring inflation expectations (a 1970s mistake) and based on mainstream thinking. Rate cuts now would offset oil based cost push inflation whereas the mainstream approach of increased rates will chase inflation higher until the economy breaks via the demand destruction channel.

  • The Result: They likely stay "higher for longer" even as growth slows, effectively "engineering" the recession to kill the demand side since they can't control the supply side (oil).

data.title: AI Abundance Won't End Inflation, Nor Make Money Meaningless
data.description:
By Peter C. Earle

Elon Musk claims AI-driven growth could fund UBI transfers without inflation. But relative prices - not just totals - still drive economic allocation.

Recent remarks by Elon Musk have reignited debate over the economic implications of artificial intelligence, following a widely circulated video clip in which he predicts a future of “universal high income” funded by direct government payments.

In the clip - shared broadly on X and quickly amplified across financial media - Musk argues that AI-driven production will expand so rapidly that it will outpace growth in the money supply, rendering such payments non-inflationary and potentially even deflationary.

As he puts it, if goods and services grow faster than money, prices should fall, even as governments distribute cash to households. The claim builds on his longstanding advocacy of income support in an AI-disrupted labor market but extends it into a more explicit monetary argument: that large-scale issuance of money need not distort prices if productivity growth is sufficiently strong.

It is a striking claim, and one that arrives at a moment when Musk’s commercial interests are increasingly tied to the perceived scale and inevitability of the AI transformation.

With his artificial intelligence initiatives becoming more deeply integrated into the broader SpaceX ecosystem - and with expectations of a major capital markets event on the horizon - there is a clear incentive to frame AI not merely as an incremental innovation but as a system-altering force capable of reshaping the global economic landscape.

That does not make the vision wrong. But it does suggest that rhetoric surrounding abundance, inevitability, and frictionless adjustment should be read, at least in part, as a forward-looking narrative - an attempt to describe not just what may happen, but what investors and the public should come to expect.

The economic reasoning underlying the claim, however, is where the argument begins to break down. Issuing money - even in a high-productivity environment - does not create income in any real sense. It redistributes claims on output.

Goods and services must still be produced. The act of distributing purchasing power does not add to that production; it just reallocates access to it. Even if AI dramatically increases the total quantity of goods available, the path by which money enters the system matters.

New money is never distributed evenly or instantaneously. It arrives through specific channels - government transfers, financial institutions, asset markets - and those entry points shape how prices adjust across sectors.

This is why the idea that inflation or deflation can be understood as a simple ratio of aggregate output to the money supply is misleading. Prices are not set in the aggregate; they are relative, reflecting the interplay of supply, demand, expectations, and timing.

When new money is introduced, it affects some prices before others, altering incentives and redirecting resources. Some sectors expand more rapidly than they otherwise would, while others are effectively taxed by rising input costs or shifting demand.

These relative price movements are not noise - they are the mechanism by which the economy coordinates activity. Distort them, and the structure of production itself becomes misaligned.

The role of monetary policy does not disappear in such a world; it may become more subtle, but no less important. If income transfers are financed by sustained monetary expansion, interest rates and credit conditions will still respond.

Artificially abundant liquidity can suppress borrowing costs and encourage investment projects that appear viable under those conditions but are not supported by underlying resource availability or consumer preferences. (Indeed, these conditions may already be manifesting.) Over time, this can lead to overextension in some sectors and underinvestment in others - a familiar pattern that has historically culminated in corrections when financial conditions tighten or expectations shift.

What is notable is how closely these latest remarks mirror Musk’s earlier statements about an AI-driven future of “sustainable abundance.” For years, he has argued that advances in automation would so dramatically expand productive capacity that scarcity itself would fade as a central economic concern.

The current formulation simply extends that logic: if scarcity recedes, then distributing money becomes a largely administrative exercise, unmoored from traditional constraints. But this is precisely where the conceptual error lies. Technology can expand what is possible - it can shift the frontier outward - but it does not eliminate the need for intertemporal coordination nor nullify the importance of how resources are allocated.

A substantial expansion in productive capacity is entirely within reach. Advances in AI could lower costs across wide swaths of the economy, streamline production, and unlock entirely new forms of output.

But greater plenty does not eliminate the need for coordination, nor does it neutralize the role of money. Prices, investment decisions, and income flows are still shaped by institutional frameworks and incentive structures, and those forces continue to operate regardless of how quickly output is growing.

If the coming decades deliver anything like the transformation being envisioned, its success will depend not only on technological capability but on how well economic systems adapt to it. Producing more with fewer inputs is a powerful development, but it does not negate the importance of sound signals in markets or disciplined allocation of capital.

Expanding the money supply alongside rising output does not bypass these considerations; it interacts with them, and if handled poorly, it can obscure rather than clarify the information that markets rely on.

If nothing else, seeing the convergence of the thinking of generational entrepreneur Elon Musk with that of NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani confirms that economists, myself included, need to do a far better job of communicating basic economic concepts.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: NAHB Housing Market Index: Builder Confidence Takes A Hit In April
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Builder confidence took a hit in April due to economic uncertainty and persistent affordability concerns. The National Association of Home Builders (NAHB) Housing Market Index (HMI) fell 4 points from March to 34 this month, marking the 24th consecutive negative reading. The latest reading was lower than the forecast of 37.

The National Association of Home Builders Housing Market Index is a gauge of builder opinion on the relative level of current and future single-family home sales. The data is collected from a monthly survey of about 900 home builders asking respondents to "rate market conditions for the sale of new homes at the present time and in the next six months as well as the traffic of prospective buyers of new homes." It is a diffusion index, which means that a reading above 50 indicates a favorable outlook on home sales; below 50 indicates a negative outlook.

Here are a few quotes from the press release:

“Builder sentiment has fallen back in spring as buyers face ongoing elevated interest rates and growing economic uncertainty,” said NAHB Chairman Bill Owens, a home builder and remodeler from Worthington, Ohio. “The year started with hopes for housing momentum growth, but risks with respect to the Iran war, energy costs, and declines for consumer confidence have slowed the market.”

“With oil prices higher in the U.S., 62% of builders reported suppliers have increased building material costs due to higher fuel prices, including gas and diesel,” said NAHB Chief Economist Robert Dietz. “Energy costs make up approximately 4% of residential construction material input and service costs. With near-term economic risks elevated, 70% of builders reported challenges pricing homes given uncertainty about material costs.”

Here is the historical series, which dates from 1985.

Components of the NAHB Housing Market Index

The NAHB Housing Market Index is calculated based off of three components: current sales, expected sales in the next six months, and traffic of prospective buyers. In April, current sales jumped to their highest level since January 2025, while future sales and buyer traffic retreated.

Housing Market Index: Regional Breakdown

The chart below gives a regional breakdown of the NAHB HMI in four regions of the country: Northeast, Midwest, South, and West.

Housing Market Index and Consumer Attitudes

The HMI correlates fairly closely with broad measures of consumer confidence. Here is a pair of overlays with the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index.

ETFs associated with home builders include: Invesco Dynamic Building & Construction ETF (PKB), iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF (ITB) and SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fair Isaac: An Ace In The Hole Overlooked By The Market
data.description:
I rate Fair Isaac Corporation (FICO) a Buy. I think the market is completely missing the forest for the trees here. Investors are so spooked by the "triple threat" of regulation, new competitors, and AI disruption that they are ignoring a massive fundamental gap. This fear has sparked a brutal sell-off, dragging the stock down over 55% from its late-2024 peak to its current level near USD 1,070.

In this analysis, I want to show why these fears are mostly noise. FICO's management is already moving to neutralize these risks through an aggressive, high-margin strategic pivot. Most retail investors still think FICO is just a credit score company. It’s not. It is the universal language of global finance—a standard with switching costs that are frankly much higher than the current stock price suggests.

The urgency for investors is underscored by two major announcements this week. First, on April 16, 2026, FICO announced it will report its Q2 FY 2026 financial results on April 28, 2026. This upcoming call will be a critical pulse check on the company's aggressive SaaS transition. Second, the company recently priced USD 1.0 billion in Senior Notes, providing the firepower for its massive USD 1.5 billion share repurchase program. Management is essentially front-running the market, signaling that today’s price is a gift for long-term buyers.

With shares trading at roughly 24x forward earnings—levels we haven't seen in nearly a decade—the disconnect between FICO’s record Q1 FY2026 numbers and the share price is just too large to ignore. For a long-term investor, this is a rare entry point.

Let’s dive in.

Context

FICO’s Q1 FY 2026 results showed a healthy 16.3% top-line jump, but you have to look closer to see what's actually driving the bus. Most of the heavy lifting came from the Scores segment (both B2B and B2C), which surged nearly 30% year-over-year. Scores now make up about 60% of total revenue. On the flip side, the software side looks sluggish at first glance, posting just 1.57% growth.

Don’t let that software number fool you—it’s a total "optical drag." This tepid headline growth is actually the result of a deliberate, aggressive move to shift the business model. Under the hood, the numbers tell a different story: the high-priority platform (SaaS) business exploded by 37%, but that was masked by a 13% drop in the legacy, non-platform segment. This is exactly what a healthy transition looks like.

Operating earnings were up a solid 30%, though EPS growth trailed at just 7.6%. This gap isn't an operational failure; it's a math problem. FICO is dealing with higher interest expenses after issuing USD 1.5 billion in senior notes back in FY 2025. They traded in old debt for new obligations at the higher rates we’re seeing across the macro landscape.

Even with those interest rate headwinds, FICO managed to beat market expectations on both the top and bottom lines. More importantly, management isn't backing down—they’ve officially reiterated their FY 2026 guidance:

FICO is eyeing USD 2.35 billion in revenue for FY 2026, which would be a solid 18% jump over last year—a clear sign the business is actually picking up speed. While that forecast is already impressive, the real takeaway from the Q1 FY 2026 call was management's tone. They sounded remarkably confident about their ability to beat these numbers, suggesting this guidance is likely a conservative floor rather than a ceiling:

Steven Weber

Executive VP & CFO

Yes. Thanks, Manav. It's a good question. We're pretty confident we're going to be able to beat our guidance. I know we talked about it was pretty conservative last quarter. At this point, we're only 3 months in. There's just a lot of questions out in the macro environment. I mean, with the Fed today, it's just -- frankly, we don't probably know what numbers we would move to. So I think by next quarter, we'll have a much better idea of what the world looks like and what overall volumes are going to look like. So I think that was our thinking behind that.

Before jumping into the valuation, we need to look at how FICO actually makes its money—that’s where you’ll see why this volatility is such a gift for long-term buyers.

Business Model

FICO’s core mission is actually pretty straightforward: they use data to change how businesses make decisions. Today, the company operates as a high-margin, asset-light powerhouse that functions as the essential connective tissue between bank data and risk management.

They’ve essentially taken their legendary credit-scoring brand and hard-wired it into a massive cloud-native software suite. This allows the world’s biggest lenders and insurance providers to automate complex decisions in real time. We’re talking about a scale that is honestly difficult for competitors to replicate.

The Scores segment is still the heavy lifter, bringing in 60% of total revenue. It is the undisputed industry standard for credit in the U.S. This business works through two channels: B2B, where scores are baked into bank transactions every day, and B2C, where consumers pay for myFICO.com. At the end of the day, this segment is a financial tollbooth. It generates a massive stream of cash because it provides a "risk language" that the global credit market simply cannot function without.

Then you have the Software segment, which makes up the other 40%. This is the real growth engine. It isn’t just generic code; these are mission-critical tools for things like fraud detection and account origination. The centerpiece is the FICO Platform—a cloud-based (SaaS) setup that lets banks build their own custom workflows.

FICO is currently forcing a massive strategic pivot here. They are walking away from old-school, on-premise setups to move everyone onto this unified platform. It’s a shift from lumpy, one-off sales to high-retention, recurring revenue. Essentially, FICO is moving from a company that sells specialized tools to one that owns the entire decisioning ecosystem

The 10-K reality: a strong moat

I spent some time deconstructing FICO's recent 10-K filings to build out a few specific performance ratios. These metrics really get under the skin of the company's operations and show the true quality of the business:

Looking at the table above, the value creation strategy here is undeniable. We are seeing a climbing ROIC fueled by expanding FCF and gross margins, all supported by consistent double-digit revenue growth. Interestingly, R&D as a percentage of gross margin has actually trended down. This tells me FICO is scaling its profitability without having to dump disproportionate capital back into R&D—a clear sign of an efficient moat.

One thing that jumped out at me was the total debt-to-FCF ratio of roughly 4x. While that implies the debt could be cleared in four years—which isn't alarming—I usually expect a "moat" company of this caliber to sit closer to 1x. After a deep dive into the cash flow statements, the reason is clear: FICO is aggressively borrowing to fund share buybacks.

In FY 2025, they tapped the market for USD 1.5 billion in senior notes at 6%. On paper, the math looks tight. If you take the FY 2025 EPS against the stock price at that time, you get a yield of about 1.54%. It seems counterintuitive to borrow at a net cost of 4% (post-tax) to buy back stock yielding 1.54%. However, FICO’s diluted EPS grew nearly 30% last year. If they maintain even a fraction of that pace, the internal return will eventually dwarf the cost of debt. I’m generally not a fan of debt-funded buybacks at these valuations, but it shows management is incredibly bullish on their own double-digit EPS growth.

Finding a company that simultaneously grows ROIC, FCF margins, and gross margins for eight straight years is incredibly rare. Critics argue that FICO is just a score that can be swapped out. They’re wrong. FICO is a language. When a bank sells a loan, the investor knows exactly what a "720 FICO" represents.

Replacing it would require the entire financial ecosystem—from regulators to global investors—to learn a new dialect overnight. It won't happen. Furthermore, their software is "hard-wired" into bank IT systems. Swapping out FICO’s decisioning engine is like trying to perform a heart transplant on a marathon runner mid-race; it’s too risky and too expensive.

Even in a recession, FICO stays relevant. When the economy sour, lenders get nervous and rely even more on precise scoring to dodge defaults. It’s likely the last software a bank would ever turn off because it’s their main defense against credit losses.

Now that we've covered the core business, let’s look at why I'm so bullish—but first, I need to address the specific "market fears" that have been weighing on the stock.

Market Fear #1: Regulatory Challenges are likely Exaggerated

To gauge the real regulatory risk here, you have first to map out how the players in this ecosystem actually interact. You have the "Big Three" bureaus—Equifax (EFX), Experian (EXPGY), and TransUnion (TRU)—acting as the raw data engines. Then come the resellers, firms like MeridianLink or Factual Data, who "package" this data into reports for lenders. For everything except mortgages, a FICO score is standard; for mortgages, regulation currently mandates both FICO and the bureau-owned VantageScore.

Historically, FICO was somewhat buried inside the bureaus' servers. They provided the software, and the bureaus handled the pricing to the end-users. That changed in October 2025 with FICO’s Mortgage Direct License Program (MDLP). By shifting the workflow directly to resellers, FICO effectively cut out the middleman and reclaimed the power to set its own prices.

This move was a direct response to the FHFA’s push for a "bi-merge" (using two bureaus instead of three). Originally, the regulator wanted to cut costs by trimming a bureau out of the process, but that would have slashed FICO’s per-loan revenue by a third. Predictably, the bureaus panicked and pushed their own VantageScore as a cheaper alternative to protect their shrinking pie.

In the end, the FHFA backed down on the bi-merge but kept the "tri-merge" requirement while including VantageScore alongside FICO. The irony is classic: a policy designed to lower costs actually made mortgage reports more expensive because lenders now have to buy both scores. FICO seized the moment, hiked prices from a range of USD 0.60–USD 3.50 up to USD 10 per score, and moved to direct licensing to maintain total control.

Is a USD 10 fee on a USD 400,000 loan really "material"? I don’t think so, especially when that score is the primary defense against credit risk. While the market fears a regulatory crackdown on these price hikes, I think the FHFA has already created enough of a mess that they have little leverage left. Furthermore, FICO is using these hikes as a "carrot and stick" approach. They are charging FICO 10T prices for legacy scores, giving lenders and Government-Sponsored Enterprises (GSEs)—like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac—a massive financial incentive to hurry up and migrate to the newer, more advanced 10T model.

That is incredible pricing power. But to be fair, we should look at the absolute worst-case regulatory scenario to see if the bear case holds any water.

Regulatory Worst-Case: How Much Damage Could They Really Do?

One nightmare scenario for the bears is the FHFA slapping a price cap on FICO and making the score optional while pushing VantageScore as the default. The goal would be to strip FICO of its pricing power and lower costs for consumers. But that plan hits a massive wall immediately: the secondary market.

The reality is that banks—from the "Too Big to Fail" giants to small community lenders—don't want to hold these loans for 30 years. They need the liquidity that comes from selling them to GSEs and global investors. And those buyers don't just want any data; they want FICO. Their decades of historical models are built on that specific language.

If regulators try to force VantageScore as the primary metric, the financial system would basically grind to a halt. Large banks can't just "flip a switch." They would have to prove to the OCC and the Fed that a new score predicts defaults just as accurately as the old one across every economic cycle. Most mid-size and community banks don't even have the quantitative teams to handle that kind of "Model Risk Management" headache. Under federal Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) guidelines, the due diligence required to switch vendors is an operational mountain they aren't staffed to climb.

So, even if a regulator labels FICO "optional," the need for seamless loan saleability makes a transition a non-starter for most lenders. The odds are heavily in FICO’s favor here. Ironically, if the government tries to interfere, they’d likely just end up causing a mess for the very borrowers they're trying to help.

What about a hard price cap? If governing bodies decide FICO’s "toll-booth" is predatory and mandate a maximum fee, it would certainly sting. It would hit the highest-margin revenue stream and likely trigger a valuation de-rating. But again, this is unlikely. Global investors buying mortgage-backed securities expect the FICO standard. Forcing an unproven alternative could cause those investors to demand a risk premium, which would ironically drive mortgage rates higher.

Even in a worst-case "cap" scenario, FICO has a massive insurance policy: the FICO Platform. If they can’t charge per score, they will simply accelerate their move to enterprise SaaS subscriptions. By embedding their analytics directly into a bank's core IT infrastructure, they can recapture revenue through software fees. These enterprise contracts are far more complex and much harder for a regulator to "cap" than a standardized credit score.

Of course, a price cap wouldn't be painless. In the short term, it would definitely create a revenue air pocket since the entire financial system isn't yet fully migrated to the FICO Platform. However, FICO isn't a passive observer here; they have one of the most effective government relations arms in the industry. Even in an extreme scenario where a cap is signed into law, FICO’s ability to influence the implementation timeline is massive.

They can effectively slow down the "velocity" of these changes, dragging out the transition period for years. This gives the company the necessary runway to finish embedding its software into the bank's core systems, effectively moving the goalposts before the regulator can even blow the whistle.

Market Fear #2: Why the AI Disruption Threat is Mostly a Myth

The big bear case recently was that AI-native firms, like Upstart, would use machine learning to cherry-pick "better" borrowers than FICO could. The theory was that AI can digest millions of alternative data points—rent, utilities, even social media—to out-predict a standard three-digit score. But this narrative misses the fact that FICO has spent the last few years turning itself into a cloud-native SaaS powerhouse to neutralize this exact threat.

The real story in 2026 is the rapid takeover of the FICO Platform. This isn't just a scoring engine anymore; it’s an integrated cloud environment. As of early 2026, Platform ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) is up 33% year-over-year, with a customer base of 150 and record software bookings hitting USD 102 million in 2025. Major players like MeridianLink and Xactus are jumping on board because this system lets them kill off their old, siloed decisioning tools and move everything into one scalable workflow.

The genius of the platform lies in its "Decision Intelligence." FICO has its own "Responsible AI" and Focused Sequence Models (FSMs), but they’ve kept the architecture open. This creates a clever "portal" effect. Instead of fintech startups trying to kill FICO, they’re actually fighting to get into the FICO Platform. They want to be an integrated service within a lender’s existing FICO pipeline because that’s the only way they can tap into FICO’s pre-built regulatory compliance and auditing tools.

Think about it from a bank’s perspective: a new AI startup can get through the front door much faster as a FICO "add-on" than it can by trying to rip out and replace the bank’s entire core engine. By making the platform the "operating system" for credit, FICO has essentially turned its potential disruptors into its best features.

Management has effectively turned the AI threat on its head. Instead of getting disrupted, FICO is positioning itself to be the tollbooth that every new AI model has to pass through.

Valuation

SA’s Quant ratings currently flag the stock as expensive with a "C" grade, largely because FICO is trading at a premium compared to its primary peers. However, to see if that premium is actually justified, I ran a valuation model using a set of conservative, "stress-tested" assumptions:

  • Share Count: 23.9 million (Q1 FY 2026).

  • FCF Margins: I’m modeling 30%. This is notably lower than the 5-year average of 34.28%, giving us a decent buffer if margins compress during the SaaS transition.

  • Time Horizon: A holding period through the end of 2030.

  • Revenue Growth: I’m sticking with management's 18% guidance for FY 2026, then tapering down to a steady 10% annually from 2027 through 2030.

  • Terminal P/FCF (2030): I’ve set this at 40x. While the multiple has swung wildly between 20x and 70x lately, it has consistently held above 40x for the last three years. Given the recurring nature of the platform revenue, 40x feels like a fair baseline for a double-digit grower.

  • Discount Rate: 9.5%. I usually look for 7-9% for a "moat" business like this, but I’ve hiked it to 9.5% here. This builds in an extra margin of safety to account for the ongoing noise around AI and regulators.

Based on these inputs, here is how the math shakes out:

Using these inputs, I’ve mapped out FICO’s revenue path through 2030. I stayed on the conservative side here: I took management’s 18% guidance for FY 2026 and then dialed it back to a steady 12% annual growth from 2027 through 2030. That brings us to a projected top line of USD 3,696 million by the end of the decade. Applying a 34% FCF margin to that figure gives us an estimated free cash flow of USD 1,257 million for 2030.

When you divide that cash flow by the 23.9 million shares currently outstanding, you get an FCF per share of 52.5. At a 40x multiple, we’re looking at a 2030 target price of USD 2,098 per share.

To find what that’s worth today, I discounted it back to 2026 using my 9.5% hurdle rate:

Intrinsic value = 2098/(1+discounted rate)^5.

Crunching those numbers leaves us with an approximate intrinsic value of USD 1,332.9 per share. At the current price of USD 1,069, that translates to a 24.6% margin of safety. In my view, FICO has a clear path to beat my 12% annual revenue growth assumption, especially since the broader analyst consensus is significantly more bullish on their trajectory through 2030.

Given the current "SaaS-apocalypse" sentiment and the headlines around new regulations, the stock is going to be bumpy. If you’ve decided this is a business you want to own for the long haul, the best approach is a slow, methodical accumulation. I wouldn't rush in with a full position right away. Building your stake gradually over the coming months will allow you to capitalize on the price swings we’re likely to see, which could very well offer even more attractive entry points than what we see today.

That said, even a high-conviction play needs to be stress-tested. Before you commit significant capital to a position, you should carefully weigh these specific risks.

Risks

The most immediate threat to this thesis is the sheer complexity of the cloud migration itself. This "heart transplant" of bank IT systems is inherently messy. If the integration hits a wall, the resulting friction could leave FICO exposed to AI-native disruptors while stalling FCF growth. However, Q1 2026 results suggest we’ve hit a tipping point. With Platform ARR accelerating 33% and ACV bookings hitting record highs, the market is signaling that the cost of staying on legacy infrastructure is now higher than the pain of moving to the cloud.

That said, investors must watch the "Transition Gap." FICO’s 122% Platform NRR—meaning existing clients are spending 22% more each year—is impressive, but the 91% NRR in the legacy business shows a deliberate cannibalization. The risk is twofold: if the old business erodes faster than the new platform can scale, or if platform adoption plateaus after this initial wave, FICO will struggle to justify its premium multiple. While I believe management has their hands on the levers here, a miscalculation in the migration's velocity would lead to a significant re-rating.

There is also the "multiple risk." My valuation assumes a 40x P/FCF, which is a haircut from the 50x and 78x multiples we saw in 2025 and 2024. However, if the regulatory noise doesn't fade, or if the "monopoly" narrative gains actual teeth, that 40x multiple could still be too generous. While FICO’s government relations team has successfully protected the "toll-booth" in the past, a shift in political sentiment is a variable even the best lobby can't always control.

Finally, we have to talk about the aggressive balance sheet. FICO is effectively running a "negative spread" by issuing debt at 6% to buy back stock with a 1.5% earnings yield. This is a high-conviction bet that future growth will dwarf the cost of capital. If a recession hits or the software transition stumbles, that $2.5 billion debt load becomes a heavy anchor.

Fortunately, management has been smart about the "maturity wall," pushing principal payments out to 2033 and 2034. This gives the SaaS platform nearly a decade to scale before the bill comes due. Combined with an asset-light model and the ability to offset interest costs with surgical price hikes, FICO has built a sturdy buffer. If you can stomach these specific risks and believe the mitigation strategies hold water, it becomes a question of how much room this "operating system of credit" deserves in your portfolio.

Takeaways

FICO remains a compelling play because the market's biggest fears—AI disruption and regulatory crackdowns—appear to be well-managed by the company’s pivot to a platform-centric model. While it’s possible that regulators could force a short-term price cap, the actual cost of a FICO score is such a rounding error in a $400,000 mortgage that lenders are unlikely to revolt. They aren't paying for a number; they are paying for a defensive shield against credit losses.

Even if the FHFA moves forward with a price cap, FICO has already built its "exit ramp." By accelerating the migration to its cloud-based platform, management can pivot away from per-score pricing toward enterprise software fees. This effectively "future-proofs" the revenue stream, moving it into an area where regulators have far less leverage and where AI becomes a feature of the platform rather than a threat to it.

Ultimately, the noise in the headlines creates an opportunity for the rational investor. While the "SaaS-apocalypse" and regulatory narratives dominate the news cycle, the structural moat here is actually widening. FICO isn't just surviving the transition to modern finance; it’s building the operating system for it. For those willing to look past the immediate volatility, the long-term compounding potential remains very much intact.
